input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

<current_datetime>2026-05-08T00:59:14.494Z</current_datetime>

I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
Here's how to make your page theme-aware:

## XAML — `AppThemeBinding`

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1C1C1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">

        <Label Text="Welcome"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#FFFFFF}" />

        <Label Text="This page follows the system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#BBBBBB}" />

        <Button Text="Get Started"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#512BD4, Dark=#B39DDB}" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

`AppThemeBinding` re-evaluates automatically whenever the OS theme changes — no event wiring needed.

## C# alternative — `SetAppTheme`

The equivalent in code-behind or C# markup:

```csharp
var label = new Label { Text = "Welcome", FontSize = 28 };
label.SetAppTheme(Label.TextColorProperty, Colors.Black, Colors.White);

var button = new Button { Text = "Get Started" };
button.SetAppTheme(Button.BackgroundColorProperty, Color.FromArgb("#512BD4"),
                                                    Color.FromArgb("#B39DDB"));
button.SetAppTheme(Button.TextColorProperty, Colors.White, Colors.Black);

BackgroundColor = Application.Current.RequestedTheme == AppTheme.Dark
    ? Color.FromArgb("#1C1C1E")
    : Colors.White;
// Better: use SetAppTheme on the page itself
this.SetAppTheme(BackgroundColorProperty, Colors.White, Color.FromArgb("#1C1C1E"));
```

`SetAppTheme(property, lightValue, darkValue)` is the C# equivalent of `AppThemeBinding` and reacts to theme changes the same way.

### Key points

| Concern | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Reactive** | Both approaches update live when the user toggles system dark mode. |
| **Scope** | Works on any `BindableProperty` — colors, images, styles, etc. |
| **Resource dictionaries** | For larger apps, define `Color` resources with `AppThemeBinding` in `App.xaml` and reference them with `{StaticResource}` to avoid duplication. |
| **Detect current theme** | `Application.Current.RequestedTheme` returns `AppTheme.Light`, `Dark`, or `Unspecified`. |
| **Listen for changes** | Subscribe to `Application.Current.RequestedThemeChanged` if you need custom logic beyond property binding. |
